verses.sec: Māṇḍūkya Upaniṣad — The Four States of Consciousness
verses.dev: ओमित्येतदक्षरमिदं सर्वं तस्योपव्याख्यानम् भूतं भवद्भवविष्यदिति सर्वमोङ्कार एव। यच्चान्यत्त्रिकालातीतं तदप्योङ्कार एव ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: omityetadakṣaramidaṃ sarvaṃ tasyopavyākhyānam bhūtaṃ bhavadbhavisyaditi sarvamoṅkāra eva | yaccānyattrikālātītaṃ tadapyoṅkāra eva ॥ 1 ॥
verses.trans: OM—this syllable is all this. All that is past, present, and future is indeed OM. And whatever else there is beyond the threefold time, that too is truly OM.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara asserts that OM is the phonetic representation of the entire cosmos and Brahman. There is no structural difference between the name (Abhidhana) and the named object (Abhidheya); both are manifestations of the singular substratum.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The tradition interprets OM as the primordial vibration of Vishnu (Narayana). The universe of time and space is real, existing as the divine body or 'Prakara' of the Lord, completely dependent on Him.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhvacharya explains that OM directly denotes Hari (Supreme Vishnu). The three divisions of time emphasize the absolute sovereignty of God over a real, eternally distinct universe of individual matter and souls.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Sri Aurobindo views OM as the word-manifestation of the cosmic and transcendent Divine. It represents the descent of the Infinite into the finite cosmic play of past, present, and future.

verses.sec: Māṇḍūkya Upaniṣad — The Four States of Consciousness
verses.trans: All this is certainly Brahman. This Self (Atman) is Brahman. This Self has four quarters (states).

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: This verse contains the Mahavakya 'Ayam Atma Brahma'. Shankara explains that the apparent dualism between the world, the individual, and God is collapsed; the four 'quarters' are not separate physical legs but layers of awareness masking the single Self.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The individual soul is essentially divine and shares attributes with Brahman, yet it remains distinct as a cell or mode within the organic whole of the Divine reality.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva asserts that 'All this is Brahman' means all things are under the controlling governance of Brahman. The four quarters represent four distinct forms of the Lord (Vishva, Taijasa, Prajna, Turiya) guiding the soul's consciousness.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo emphasizes this as the foundation for an all-inclusive spiritual realization: the cosmos, the individual inner mind, and the Absolute are all integrated aspects of the one Divine being.

verses.sec: Māṇḍūkya Upaniṣad — The Four States of Consciousness
verses.dev: जागरितस्थानो बहिष्प्रज्ञः सप्ताङ्ग एकोनविंशतिमुखः स्थूलभुग्वैश्वानरः प्रथमः पादः ॥ ३ ॥
verses.iast: jāgaritasthāno bahiṣprajñaḥ saptāṅga ekonaviṃśatimukhaḥ sthūlabhugvaiśvānaraḥ prathamaḥ pādaḥ ॥ 3 ॥
verses.trans: The first quarter is Vaishvanara, whose sphere of activity is the waking state, who is conscious of external objects, who has seven limbs and nineteen mouths, and who experiences the gross material world.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Vaishvanara represents collective and individual physical consciousness. The 'seven limbs' are cosmic components (heaven, sun, air, etc.) and 'nineteen mouths' are the gates of perception (senses, pranas, mind), showing that the waking state mistakenly views reality as external and material.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Vaishvanara is the Lord manifesting through the physical universe, sustaining the waking experience of souls so they may exhaust their physical karma through objective interaction.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: The Lord rules over the waking state of the individual jiva through His form as Vaishvanara. The distinct separation between the experiencing soul and the physical object is real and divinely ordered.
commentaries.integral_advaita: This represents the first layer of evolution: the physicalized consciousness. It is our outward-turned awareness focused entirely on dealing with concrete material forms.

verses.sec: Māṇḍūkya Upaniṣad — The Four States of Consciousness
verses.dev: स्वप्नस्थानोऽन्तःप्रज्ञः सप्ताङ्ग एकोनविंशतिमुखः प्रविविक्तभुक्तैजसो द्वितीयः पादः ॥ ४ ॥
verses.iast: svapnasthāno'ntaḥprajñaḥ saptāṅga ekonaviṃśatimukhaḥ praviviktabhuktaijaso dvitīyaḥ pādaḥ ॥ 4 ॥
verses.trans: The second quarter is Taijasa, whose sphere of activity is the dream state, who is conscious of internal objects, who has seven limbs and nineteen mouths, and who experiences subtle mental impressions.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Taijasa is the luminous internal mind. Shankara points out that in dreams, the mind creates a whole universe independent of physical reality using past memories, proving that objective experience is a construct of consciousness.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The dream state is a real psychological landscape created by the Lord for the individual jiva, presenting subtle subjective joys and terrors appropriate to that soul’s internal state.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: The Lord operates through His subtle form called Taijasa to govern the inner world of memory and imagination, keeping the soul distinct from its thoughts.
commentaries.integral_advaita: This corresponds to the vital and subliminal mind. It is the internal theater where the consciousness processes the vast interior world of archetypes, energies, and forces behind the physical veil.

verses.sec: Māṇḍūkya Upaniṣad — The Four States of Consciousness
verses.dev: यत्र सुप्तो न कञ्चन कामं कामयते न कञ्चन स्वप्नम्पश्यति तत्सुषुप्तम्। सुषुप्तस्थान एकीभूतः प्रज्ञानघन एवानन्दमयो ह्यानन्दभुक्चेतोमुखः प्राज्ञस्तृतीयः पादः ॥ ५ ॥
verses.iast: yatra supto na kañcana kāmaṃ kāmayate na kañcana svapnampasyati tatsuṣuptam | suṣuptasthāna ekībhūtaḥ prajñānaghana evānandamayo hyānandabhukcetomukhaḥ prājñastṛtīyah pādaḥ ॥ 5 ॥
verses.trans: The state where a sleeping person desires no object and sees no dream is deep sleep (Susupti). Consisting of unified consciousness, a mass of pure undifferentiated awareness, full of bliss and enjoying bliss, Prajna is the third quarter.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: In Susupti, duality dissolves temporarily into a homogeneous causal seed-state (Prajnanaghana). Because the ego is asleep, there is an experience of peace and 'bliss' (Anandamaya), though it is still veiled by primal ignorance (Avidya).
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Deep sleep is a state where the soul rests directly in the embrace of Brahman, safe from the turbulent currents of physical and mental modifications, recovering its vital energy.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva explains that the Lord acts as Prajna in this state. The individual soul remains active but experiences no external or internal objects; its blissful rest is entirely a gift of divine grace.
commentaries.integral_advaita: This matches the superconscient or causal layer. It is a massed potentiality of consciousness where all specific operations are absorbed into a powerful, quiet seed-state of spiritual force.

verses.sec: Māṇḍūkya Upaniṣad — The Four States of Consciousness
verses.dev: एष सर्वेश्वरः एष सर्वज्ञ एषोऽन्तर्याम्येष योनिः सर्वस्य प्रभवाप्ययौ हि भूतानाम् ॥ ६ ॥
verses.iast: eṣa sarveśvaraḥ eṣa sarvajña eṣo'ntaryāmyeṣo yoniḥ sarvasya prabhavāpyayau hi bhūtānām ॥ 6 ॥
verses.trans: This is the Lord of all; this is the knower of all; this is the inner controller; this is the source of all; this is the absolute origin and dissolution of all beings.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara clarifies that Prajna associated with the causal state is Saguna Brahman (Ishvara). It is the cosmic architect, yet from the ultimate standpoint, even this creator-god state belongs to the realm of relative reality (Maya), prior to the unconditioned Absolute.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: This verse directly describes the personal Supreme Being (Isvara). He is the eternal, independent ruler, the inner ruler (Antaryamin) of all living souls and physical matter.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva highlights this verse to firmly reinforce the absolute difference between God and creation. The Lord alone is omniscient, omnipotent, and the primary cause of cosmic manifestation and destruction.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo sees this as the Divine Matrix, the Supermind or Ishvara-consciousness from which all terms of the lower universe are systematically projected, maintained, and retrieved.

verses.sec: Māṇḍūkya Upaniṣad — The Four States of Consciousness
verses.dev: नान्तःप्रज्ञं न बहिष्प्रज्ञं नोभयतःप्रज्ञं न प्रज्ञानघनं न प्रज्ञं नाप्रज्ञम्। अदृष्टमव्यवहार्यमग्राह्यमलक्षणमचिन्त्यमव्यपदेश्यमेकात्मप्रत्ययसारं प्रपञ्चोपशमं शान्तं शिवमद्वैतं चतुर्थं मन्यन्ते स आत्मा स विज्ञेयः ॥ ७ ॥
verses.iast: nāntahprajñaṃ na bahiṣprajñaṃ nobhayataḥprajñaṃ na prajñānaghanaṃ na prajñaṃ nāprajñam | adṛṣṭamavyavahāryamagrāhyamalakṣaṇamacintyamavyapadeśyamekātmapratyayasāraṃ prapañcopśamaṃ śāntaṃ śivamadvaitaṃ caturthaṃ manyante sa ātmā sa vijñeyaḥ ॥ 7 ॥
verses.trans: Conscious neither of the internal nor of the external world, nor of both; not a mass of consciousness, neither conscious nor unconscious; unseen, un-relatable, incomprehensible, indefinable, unthinkable, indescribable; whose essence is the sole realization of the one Self; the cessation of all pluralistic phenomena; peaceful, auspicious, and non-dual—this is thought to be the fourth state (Turiya). That is the Self; That must be realized.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The core pinnacle of Advaita logic. Shankara shows that Turiya is not a 'fourth state' like the others, but the timeless reality behind them. It is defined solely via negation (Neti, Neti) because it transcends all subject-object dichotomies; it is pure, undivided Consciousness.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Turiya is the realization of the pristine soul freed from the three material states of Nature. It is the auspicious, serene dawn of the soul’s true relationship of absolute alignment with Brahman.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva interprets Turiya as the supreme transcendent aspect of Vishnu, distinct from the forms that govern waking, dreaming, and sleeping. It is the independent, blissful form achieved by liberated souls.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo describes this as the pure, static, unconditioned Transcendent. It is the absolute status of Spirit that provides the foundation for all active cosmic manifestation without being bound by it.

verses.sec: Māṇḍūkya Upaniṣad — The Four States of Consciousness
verses.dev: सोऽयमात्माध्यक्षरमोङ्कारोऽधिमात्रं पादा मात्रा मात्राश्च पादा अकार उकारो मकार इति ॥ ८ ॥
verses.iast: so'yamātmādhyakṣaramoṅkāro'dhimātraṃ pādā mātrā mātrāśca pādā akāra ukāro makāra iti ॥ 8 ॥
verses.trans: That same Self, looked at from the standpoint of the syllable, is OM. Looked at from the standpoint of letters, its quarters are the letters (elements), and the letters are the quarters: A, U, and M.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara links the structural layers of consciousness with the acoustic components of the sacred word. Meditating on the sounds allows the seeker to methodically resolve the outer universe back into the inner Spirit.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The matching of sounds to states shows a structured path of meditation designed to guide the human mind from physical perception to divine focus.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: The letters represent the graded hierarchy of cosmic forces and controllers, all nested within and controlled by the overarching power of the name of God.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo highlights this as a formula for tracking the descent of consciousness from the unmanifest into vocal sound, or conversely, ascending from sound to spirit.

verses.sec: Māṇḍūkya Upaniṣad — The Four States of Consciousness
verses.dev: जागरितस्थानो वैश्वानरोऽकारः प्रथमा मात्राऽऽप्तेरादिमत्त्वाद्वाऽऽप्नोति ह वै सर्वान्कामानादिश्च भवति य एवं वेद ॥ ९ ॥
verses.iast: jāgaritasthāno vaiśvānaro'kāraḥ prathamā mātrā''pterādimattvādvā''pnoti ha vai sarvānkāmānādiśca bhavati ya evaṃ veda ॥ 9 ॥
verses.trans: Vaishvanara, whose sphere is the waking state, is 'A', the first letter, because of its all-pervasiveness or its being first. He who knows this obtains all desires and becomes foremost.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The sound 'A' is foundational to speech, just as the waking state is foundational to human experience. Resolving 'A' means seeing the physical world as a temporal appearance.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The sound 'A' represents the source, Vishnu, initiating the cosmic creation and supporting the objective waking life of all bound entities.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva explains that 'A' signifies the form of the Lord that satisfies outer needs and grants material success to those who approach Him with pure intention.
commentaries.integral_advaita: This maps out the physicalization of the Word. It represents the outer crust of creation where spiritual light is hidden within dense material forms.

verses.sec: Māṇḍūkya Upaniṣad — The Four States of Consciousness
verses.dev: स्वप्नस्थानस्तैजस उकारो द्वितीया मात्रोत्कर्षादुभयत्त्वाद्वाोत्कर्षति ह वै ज्ञानसन्ततिं समानश्च भवति नास्याब्रह्मवित्कुले भवति य एवं वेद ॥ १० ॥
verses.iast: svapnasthanastaijasa ukāro dvitīyā mātrotkarṣādubhayattvādvāotkarṣati ha vai jñānasantatiṃ samānaśca bhavati nāsyābrahmavitkule bhavati ya evaṃ veda ॥ 10 ॥
verses.trans: Taijasa, whose sphere is dream, is 'U', the second letter, because of its intermediate position or superior quality. He who knows this elevates the lineage of knowledge, becomes balanced, and no one ignorant of Brahman is born in his family.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The sound 'U' is intermediate between 'A' and 'M', just as dream bridges the gap between waking and sleeping. Recognizing this subtle state breaks the illusion of physical stability.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The intermediate sound 'U' reminds the soul of its transition from objective distraction toward subjective inner stillness.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: The Lord as 'U' grants superior intellectual understanding and spiritual discrimination, establishing the devotee firmly in higher scriptural knowledge.
commentaries.integral_advaita: The vital and imaginative energy. 'U' is the processing dynamic where matter begins to open up, releasing its inner mental and psychological possibilities.

verses.sec: Māṇḍūkya Upaniṣad — The Four States of Consciousness
verses.dev: सुषुप्तस्थानः प्राज्ञो मकारस्तृतीया मात्रा मितेरपीतेर्वा मिनोति ह वा इदं सर्वमपीतिश्च भवति य एवं वेद ॥ ११ ॥
verses.iast: suṣuptasthānaḥ prājño makārastṛtīyā mātrā miterapītervā minoti ha vā idaṃ sarvamapītiśca bhavati ya evaṃ veda ॥ 11 ॥
verses.trans: Prajna, whose sphere is deep sleep, is 'M', the third letter, because it is the measure or the final resolution. He who knows this is able to understand all things and dissolves into the source.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: 'M' closes the lips and absorbs the preceding sounds, just as deep sleep absorbs waking and dreaming. This realization demonstrates that the entire universe returns to a singular causal potentiality.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The resolution into 'M' represents the soul being drawn back into a state of quiet rest within the Sovereign Creator, awaiting the next dawn of service.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: The Lord as 'M' grants final closure to mundane worldly pursuits, measuring out the karmic destiny of souls and preparing them for true devotion.
commentaries.integral_advaita: The unmanifest seed-state. It is the absorption of all outer and inner mental activity into a massive, concentrated silence of spiritual potential.

verses.sec: Māṇḍūkya Upaniṣad — The Four States of Consciousness
verses.dev: अमात्रश्चतुर्थोऽव्यवहार्यः प्रपञ्चोपशमः शिवोऽद्वैत एवमेवमोङ्कार आत्मैव संविशत्यात्मनाऽऽत्मानं य एवं वेद ॥ १२ ॥
verses.iast: amātraścaturtho'vyavahāryaḥ prapañcopāśamaḥ śivo'dvaita evamevamoṅkāra ātmaiva saṃviśatyātmanā''tmānaṃ ya evaṃ veda ॥ 12 ॥
verses.trans: The partless OM is the fourth state (Turiya)—beyond words, the cessation of all change, auspicious, and completely non-dual. Thus, the syllable OM is truly the Self. He who knows this merges his individual self into the supreme Self.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The silent interval after OM fades is the 'Amatra' (soundless/measureless). Shankara emphasizes this as the ultimate liberation: the illusion of a separate ego is destroyed, and pure, undivided Consciousness remains as the absolute truth.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The silence signifies the soul entering an eternal, pristine communion with the Divine Lord, retaining its distinct nature but experiencing total freedom from material bondages.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: The soundless conclusion represents the unimaginable majesty of Sri Hari, where the liberated soul enters a permanent state of bliss, ever conscious of its distinction from the Infinite Master.
commentaries.integral_advaita: The comprehensive realization where the silence and the sound, the unmanifest Absolute and the dynamic universe, are known as one single, beautifully integrated Divine Consciousness.
